modules.title: Greetings & Basics
modules.description: Learn essential greetings and polite expressions

lessons.title: Hello & Goodbye
lessons.culturalNote: In Maharashtra, 'Namaskar' with folded hands is the universal greeting, preferred over 'Namaste'. It works at any time of day and with anyone regardless of age or status.

phrases.usage: Common greeting, slightly less formal than Namaskar

lessons.title: Please & Thank You
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi culture values politeness deeply. While 'Krupayaa' (please) exists, politeness is often conveyed through verb forms and tone. 'Dhanyavaad' is universally understood for thanks.

lessons.title: Sorry & Excuse Me
lessons.culturalNote: Apologizing in Marathi is often accompanied by body language - a slight bow or touching elder's feet. 'Maaf karaa' is the everyday apology; 'Kshamaa karaa' is more literary and formal.

phrases.usage: Informal apology to friends or younger people
When asking someone to move aside

lessons.title: Yes, No & Basic Responses
lessons.culturalNote: In Marathi, 'Ho' (yes) and 'Naahi' (no) are the basic responses. The famous Indian head wobble (side-to-side) often means 'yes' or acknowledgment in Maharashtra.

lessons.title: Basic Courtesy
lessons.culturalNote: Maharashtrian hospitality is legendary. Offering water and tea to guests is an essential custom. The saying 'Paahune dev asataat' (guests are gods) reflects the deep cultural value of hospitality.

phrases.usage: Offering tea, a must in Maharashtra
Said when someone leaves your home

phrases.english: I felt good / That was nice

lessons.title: Formal Speech
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi has distinct formal (tumhi/aapan) and informal (too) registers. Using the formal register with elders, strangers, and in professional settings is crucial. The wrong register can cause offense.
lessons.grammarFocus: Formal speech uses 'tumhi' (you-formal) or 'aapan' (you-very formal) with verb conjugations ending in '-aa' or '-aat'. Example: 'Tumhi kase aahaat?' (How are you?-formal).

phrases.usage: Respectful inquiry to elders or dignitaries
Polite inquiry to equals or strangers

lessons.title: Informal Speech
lessons.culturalNote: Informal Marathi uses 'tu' (you-informal) with '-s' verb endings. Used among close friends, with younger people, and within family among same-age members. Using this with elders is disrespectful.
lessons.grammarFocus: Informal conjugations use '-s' endings: 'too kasaa aahes?' (how are you? to male friend), 'too kashi aahes?' (to female friend). The '-s' marks second person singular informal.

lessons.title: Titles & Honorifics
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi uses respectful suffixes and titles extensively. Adding 'ji' or 'saheb' to names shows respect. Family terms like 'kaka' (uncle) and 'tai' (elder sister) are used even for non-relatives.

phrases.usage: Respectful address for men in formal settings
Respectful address for women in formal settings
Respectful term for older males, even strangers
For older men, relatives or strangers alike
For older women, relatives or strangers
Common in rural Maharashtra, very respectful

lessons.title: Polite Requests
lessons.culturalNote: Polite requests in Marathi use the formal imperative: verb stem + '-aa'. Adding 'krupayaa' makes it extra polite, though Marathi relies more on verb form and tone than a separate 'please' word.
lessons.grammarFocus: Polite imperative uses formal 'tumhi' form: verb stem + '-aa'. Examples: 'dyaa' (please give), 'saangaa' (please tell), 'yaa' (please come).

lessons.title: Common Responses
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi responses often include particles like 'naa', 'ki', and 'baram' that add nuance and warmth. Learning these filler words helps you sound natural in conversation.

lessons.title: Blessings & Well-wishes
lessons.culturalNote: Blessings are integral to Marathi culture. Elders bless younger people by placing a hand on their head. Seeking blessings (aashirvaad) from elders before important events is a cherished tradition.

phrases.english: May God give you long life

phrases.usage: Blessing for success, often at exams

phrases.english: May you live a hundred years
May God's blessings be on you

lessons.title: Meeting Someone New
lessons.culturalNote: When meeting someone new in Maharashtra, people join hands in Namaskar. Handshakes are common in urban professional settings. Asking about family and origin early in conversation is normal and not intrusive.

lessons.title: Phone Greetings
lessons.culturalNote: Phone calls in Maharashtra often begin with 'Hello' (borrowed from English) followed by Marathi. Ending calls politely is important - abruptly hanging up is considered rude. Always greet before stating your purpose.

lessons.title: Leave-taking & Farewells
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi farewells are often elaborate. Hosts walk guests to the door or vehicle. Simply leaving without proper goodbye is impolite. 'Yeto/Yete' literally means 'I come' but is used as 'I'll be going now'.

lessons.title: Everyday Expressions
lessons.culturalNote: These everyday expressions are the backbone of daily Marathi conversation. Heard constantly in homes, streets, and workplaces across Maharashtra. Mastering these makes you sound natural.

phrases.usage: Very common reaction to any event

modules.title: Introducing Yourself
modules.description: Learn to introduce yourself and share personal details

lessons.title: Saying Your Name
lessons.culturalNote: In Maharashtra, full introductions include first name, father's first name, and surname. This tradition reflects the deep importance of family identity. Surnames often indicate caste, community, or region.

phrases.english: How do you spell the name?
phrases.usage: Asking someone to spell their name
Formal phrase when introducing two people

lessons.title: Asking & Telling Age
lessons.culturalNote: Asking someone's age is more acceptable in Indian culture than in the West. It helps determine the appropriate level of respect and formality. However, directly asking a woman's age can still be impolite.
lessons.grammarFocus: Age in Marathi uses 'varshe' (years): 'Malaa pachvees varshe aahet' (I am 25 years old). Gender affects the adjective: '-chaa' for males, '-chi' for females.

lessons.title: Where Are You From
lessons.culturalNote: Maharashtra is a large state with distinct regional identities. People from Mumbai, Pune, Kolhapur, Vidarbha, and Konkan each have different dialects. Asking origin often reveals cultural background.

lessons.title: Your Profession
lessons.culturalNote: Profession is a common introductory topic in Maharashtra. Pune's IT industry and Mumbai's business/film world make 'engineer' and 'businessman' very frequent answers. Asking is considered friendly, not intrusive.

lessons.title: Family Members
lessons.culturalNote: Family is central to Marathi culture. Marathi has very specific kinship terms - different words for maternal vs paternal relatives. Extended family is important and you will be asked about family early in any acquaintance.

lessons.title: Marital Status
lessons.culturalNote: Marriage is a significant social institution in Maharashtra. Questions about marital status are common and not considered intrusive. Both arranged and love marriages are practiced widely.

phrases.english: We got married two years ago

lessons.title: Languages Spoken
lessons.culturalNote: Maharashtra is multilingual. Most Marathis speak Marathi, Hindi, and some English. Mumbai residents often speak Gujarati or Urdu too. Being multilingual is highly valued and common.
lessons.grammarFocus: The verb 'yete/yetam' (comes/to come) is used idiomatically for knowing a language: 'Malaa Maraathi yetam' literally means 'Marathi comes to me' = 'I know Marathi'.

phrases.english: How do you say this in Marathi?

lessons.title: Education
lessons.culturalNote: Education is highly valued in Maharashtra. Social reformers like Mahatma Phule and Savitribai Phule championed education for all. Asking about education is a normal part of getting to know someone.

phrases.english: I studied in the science stream

lessons.title: Likes & Dislikes
lessons.culturalNote: Expressing preferences is key for social bonding. In Maharashtra, food preferences, cricket opinions, and movie tastes are the most common topics for sharing likes and dislikes.
lessons.grammarFocus: Likes use 'aavaDte/aavaDtam' (it pleases): 'Malaa cricket aavaDtam' (I like cricket). Dislikes negate it: 'Malaa ... aavaDat naahi' (I don't like ...). The subject that likes takes '-laa'.

phrases.usage: Basic way to express a preference

lessons.title: Hobbies
lessons.culturalNote: Popular hobbies in Maharashtra include cricket, reading Marathi literature, gardening, cooking traditional dishes, and watching Marathi theatre. Lavani and Bhajan are beloved musical traditions.

lessons.title: Daily Life
lessons.culturalNote: A typical Maharashtrian day starts early with morning tea. Breakfast varies by region. Lunch is the main meal with bhaakar/chapati and bhaji. Evening chai is a cherished ritual. Dinner is usually lighter.

phrases.english: I go for an evening walk

lessons.title: Describing Home
lessons.culturalNote: Homes in Maharashtra range from traditional 'wadas' (courtyard houses) to modern apartments. Mumbai's iconic 'chawls' are shared housing buildings. In villages, houses often have a central 'angan' (courtyard).

phrases.english: My house is on the first floor
There is a temple near the house
Our house is in a quiet area

lessons.title: Neighborhood
lessons.culturalNote: Neighborhoods in Maharashtra have distinct characters. In cities, 'peths' are old market areas named after days or commodities (like Mangalwaar Peth). Close-knit 'galli' (lane) culture fosters strong community bonds.

phrases.english: There is lots of greenery here
The bus stop is close by

lessons.title: Personal Qualities
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi culture values honesty (praamaaNikpaNaa), hard work (mehnat), and humility (namrataa). Self-praise is generally avoided in traditional settings - ideally others describe your good qualities.

lessons.title: Future Plans
lessons.culturalNote: In Marathi culture, future plans are often discussed with family. Major decisions are made collectively. 'Dev paavlaa tar' (if God is willing) is commonly added when discussing future plans.
lessons.grammarFocus: Future desires use '-aayacham aahe' construction: 'Malaa shikaayacham aahe' (I want to study). This attaches to the verb stem and expresses intent or desire.

phrases.marathi: मला स्वतःचा व्यवसाय सुरू करायचा आहे
phrases.transliteration: Malaa svataahchaa vyavasaay suroo karaychaa aahe
phrases.english: I want to start my own business
In the future I want to become a doctor

modules.title: Numbers & Time
modules.description: Master counting, dates, telling time, and schedules

lessons.title: Numbers 1-10
lessons.culturalNote: Counting in Marathi uses unique Devanagari numerals. Standard counting is used for items, money, and time.

lessons.title: Numbers 11-20
lessons.culturalNote: Numbers from 11 to 20 have unique names in Marathi. Practicing these is essential for transactions and telephone numbers.

lessons.title: Tens & 100
lessons.culturalNote: Counting in tens (30, 40, 50...) is crucial for prices and counting larger quantities. 'Shambhar' is 100.

lessons.title: Ordinals & Fractions
lessons.culturalNote: Fractions like 'Paav' (quarter), 'Ardhaa' (half), and 'Paun' (three-quarters) are heavily used in vegetable markets.

lessons.title: Asking the Time
lessons.culturalNote: Asking the time is usually polite. Use 'kiti' (how many/much) and 'vaajle' (struck/sounded).

phrases.english: What is the time by your watch?
How much time will it take?

lessons.title: Telling the Time
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi uses 'vaajle' to say it is X o'clock. For half past, use 'saaDe'. For quarter past, 'savvaa'. For quarter to, 'pauNe'.

lessons.title: Parts of the Day
lessons.culturalNote: Understanding parts of the day helps in setting appointments and planning routines.

lessons.title: Days of the Week
lessons.culturalNote: Days of the week end in '-vaar'. Knowing them is vital for plans and schedules.

lessons.title: Months & Seasons
lessons.culturalNote: Maharashtra has three primary seasons: UnhaLaa (summer), Pausaala (monsoon), and Hivaala (winter).

phrases.english: Diwali is in October or November

lessons.title: Relative Time
lessons.culturalNote: Terms like 'today', 'tomorrow', 'yesterday' are essential for basic storytelling and scheduling.

phrases.english: Day before yesterday / Day after tomorrow
phrases.usage: Context decides past or future (2 days away)

lessons.title: Frequency Adverbs
lessons.culturalNote: Adverbs like 'always' and 'never' clarify your habits and preferences.

lessons.title: Duration
lessons.culturalNote: Units of time like minutes and hours are useful for quantifying waiting times.

phrases.english: It will take me two hours
He is on leave for three days

lessons.title: Timelines & Schedules
lessons.culturalNote: Talking about when things start, end, or when you are free.

lessons.title: Date & Year
lessons.culturalNote: Asking for or stating specific calendar dates.

lessons.title: Age & Milestones
lessons.culturalNote: Discussing age, longevity, and key life milestones.

phrases.english: My son is ten years old

modules.title: At the Market
modules.description: Shop for groceries, vegetables, bargaining and paying

lessons.title: Finding a Shop
lessons.culturalNote: Asking where to buy daily necessities. 'Kuthe' is the keyword for 'where'.

lessons.title: Shop Greetings
lessons.culturalNote: Starting a friendly exchange with a shopkeeper (dukaandaar).

lessons.title: Asking for Prices
lessons.culturalNote: The most fundamental market phrase. 'Kiti' means how much.

lessons.title: Bargaining
lessons.culturalNote: Bargaining is common in street markets in Maharashtra. Be polite but firm. Use 'thodaa' (a little) and 'kami karaa' (reduce).

phrases.english: Please reduce the price a little
Will you give it for 50 rupees?

lessons.title: Quantities & Weights
lessons.culturalNote: Ordering vegetables by weight: 'Kilo' and 'Gram' are standard.

phrases.english: Give me one kilo of potatoes

lessons.title: Fruits & Vegetables
lessons.culturalNote: Common produce terms in Maharashtra. Mangoes (Haapoos) are highly celebrated.

lessons.title: Groceries & Staples
lessons.culturalNote: Common ingredients in Maharashtrian kitchens: rice, oil, wheat flour.

lessons.title: Clothes Shopping
lessons.culturalNote: Shopping for traditional wear like Sarees or Kurta, or modern wear.

phrases.english: I want to buy a saree

lessons.title: Colors & Sizes
lessons.culturalNote: Specifying what style, color, or fit you want.

phrases.english: Show it to me in red

lessons.title: Quality & Condition
lessons.culturalNote: Inspecting items for defects or ensuring high quality.

phrases.english: Is there any defect in this?

lessons.title: Paying / Cash / Card
lessons.culturalNote: Handling transaction methods. Digital payments (UPI / GPay) are highly prevalent in India now.

phrases.english: Can I pay via Google Pay (UPI)?
I have sent the money online

lessons.title: Carrying / Bags
lessons.culturalNote: Carrying items. Plastic bags are banned/restricted in Maharashtra; cloth bags (kaaPdee pishvee) are preferred.

phrases.english: Please do not give a plastic bag
Help me carry this to the vehicle

lessons.title: Returning & Exchanging
lessons.culturalNote: Dealing with product returns or exchange conditions.

phrases.english: I didn't like this after going home
Is there a discount on this?

lessons.title: Street Vendors
lessons.culturalNote: Interacting with roadside carts ('haatgaadee') and vendors.

phrases.usage: Polite address to elderly female vendor

lessons.title: Shopping Complete
lessons.culturalNote: Concluding your shopping trip with polite thanks.

phrases.english: It was a very nice experience

modules.title: Food & Dining
modules.description: Ordering food, specifying tastes, kitchen vocabulary, and paying

lessons.title: Ordering Food
lessons.culturalNote: Traditional Maharashtrian restaurants serve 'Thali' (meals on a plate). Asking for menus and recommending items.

phrases.english: Please give me the menu card
Give me one plate of Misal Pav

lessons.title: Taste & Preferences
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi food can range from mild to very spicy. Words like 'tikhat' (spicy) and 'god' (sweet) are essential.

phrases.marathi: मला कमी तिखट जेवण हवं आहे
phrases.transliteration: Malaa kami tikhat jevaN havam aahe
phrases.english: I want a less spicy meal
I do not like spicy/greasy food

lessons.title: Table Requests
lessons.culturalNote: Asking for utensils, condiments, or extra servings.

phrases.english: I've been waiting for an hour

lessons.title: In the Kitchen
lessons.culturalNote: Understanding cooking verbs and kitchen vocabulary.

lessons.title: Paying the Food Bill
lessons.culturalNote: Concluding your dining experience with the bill request and tipping.

phrases.english: Make the payment with my card
The food was wonderful, thank you

modules.title: Directions & Travel
modules.description: Asking directions, taking public transit, buying tickets

lessons.title: Asking Directions
lessons.culturalNote: Finding landmarks in Maharashtra. Marathi addresses often use 'peths' (areas) and landmarks like temples.

phrases.english: Please show me on the map

lessons.title: Giving Directions
lessons.culturalNote: Understanding directions given by locals. 'Davi' is left, 'Ujavi' is right.

phrases.english: The shop is on the left side
You will see a temple in front

lessons.title: Buying Tickets
lessons.culturalNote: Purchasing transit tickets for local trains (like Mumbai Locals) or state transport (MSRTC) buses.

phrases.english: Give me a ticket to Mumbai
How many tickets do you need?
How much is the ticket fare?
Where can I get a pass?

lessons.title: Modes of Transport
lessons.culturalNote: Identifying modes of transportation. 'Auto' or 'Rickshaw' is the most popular for short distances.

phrases.english: Does the rickshaw run on meter?

lessons.title: Travel Inquiries
lessons.culturalNote: Asking about schedules, delays, and routes.

phrases.english: When will the next train leave?
Does this bus go to Pune?
How many hours is the journey?

modules.title: Daily Routine
modules.description: Morning to night habits, chores, work and office routines

lessons.title: Morning Routine
lessons.culturalNote: Describing what you do in the morning. Use present habitual tense.

phrases.english: I wake up early in the morning (male)
I wake up early in the morning (female)

lessons.title: Household Chores
lessons.culturalNote: Talking about housekeeping activities around the house.

lessons.title: Evening & Bedtime
lessons.culturalNote: Winding down the day and preparing for bed.

phrases.english: I fell into a deep sleep

lessons.title: Work & Office Routine
lessons.culturalNote: Describing a typical professional workday.

phrases.marathi: माझे ऑफिस नऊ वाजता सुरू होते
phrases.transliteration: Maajhe office nau vaajataa suroo hote

phrases.marathi: मी पाच वाजता लॉग ऑफ करतो
phrases.transliteration: Mee paach vaajataa log off karto

lessons.title: Weekend Habits
lessons.culturalNote: What you do during holidays or weekends (weekly off).

phrases.english: On Saturdays I wake up late (male)

modules.title: People & Descriptions
modules.description: Physical attributes, personality, emotions, family and friends

lessons.title: Physical Appearance
lessons.culturalNote: Describing what someone looks like. Height, hair, and posture.

lessons.title: Describing Personality
lessons.culturalNote: Describing inner nature (svabhaav). 'Svabhaav' is a very key cultural word.

lessons.title: Expressing Emotions
lessons.culturalNote: Saying how you feel. Use present state verbs.

phrases.english: I am bored / tired of this

lessons.title: Relations & Friends
lessons.culturalNote: Describing family relations and close friends.

lessons.title: Comparisons
lessons.culturalNote: Comparing two people or things using 'pekshaa' (compared to).

phrases.english: This house is the most beautiful

modules.title: Health & Emergencies
modules.description: Describing illness, doctor visits, buying medicines, asking for help

lessons.title: Feeling Unwell
lessons.culturalNote: Describing general physical discomfort. In Marathi, body conditions are often described with 'malaa... hotay' (to me... is happening).

lessons.title: At the Doctor
lessons.culturalNote: Interacting with medical professionals. Explaining pain and symptoms.

phrases.english: I want to see the doctor

phrases.marathi: हे औषध दिवसातून तीन वेळा घ्या
phrases.transliteration: He aushadh divasaatoon teen veLaa ghyaa
phrases.english: Take this medicine three times a day

lessons.title: At the Pharmacy
lessons.culturalNote: Buying medicines (aushadh) and understanding dosage instructions.

phrases.english: What is the expiry date of this medicine?

lessons.title: Emergencies & Accidents
lessons.culturalNote: Calling emergency services or dealing with roadside accidents.

lessons.title: Asking for Help
lessons.culturalNote: Shouting for help or seeking assistance from passersby.

phrases.marathi: काळजी करू नका, मी मदत करतो
phrases.transliteration: KaaLji karoo nakaa, mee madat karto

modules.title: Social Conversations
modules.description: Weather talks, hobbies, inviting friends, small talk

lessons.title: Weather & Climate
lessons.culturalNote: Weather is a popular small talk topic. Maharashtra experiences heavy rains (monsoon) and scorching summers.

phrases.english: Clouds have gathered in the sky

lessons.title: Hobbies & Free Time
lessons.culturalNote: Sharing what you love to do in your leisure hours.

phrases.english: I like to listen to songs

lessons.title: Likes & Dislikes
lessons.culturalNote: Expressing preferences using 'aavaDtan' (liked) and 'aavaDat naahi' (disliked).

phrases.english: I like Puranpoli (sweet flatbread) very much

lessons.title: Inviting Friends
lessons.culturalNote: Inviting someone to your home or for an event. Hospitalities are key.

phrases.english: We are hosting a party on Sunday

lessons.title: Polite Small Talk
lessons.culturalNote: Essential phrases for maintaining a casual, polite conversation.

phrases.marathi: मला तुमची गप्पा मारून आनंद झाला
phrases.transliteration: Malaa tumchee gappaa maaroon aanand jhaalaa

modules.title: Work & Business
modules.description: Office communication, phone calls, meetings, appointments

lessons.title: In the Office
lessons.culturalNote: Typical workspace interactions and basic administrative needs.

phrases.english: There is a lot of work today

lessons.title: Professional Introductions
lessons.culturalNote: Introducing yourself in formal corporate or professional settings.

lessons.title: Phone Conversations
lessons.culturalNote: Answering calls, verifying caller identity, and taking messages.

lessons.title: Meetings & Discussions
lessons.culturalNote: Common corporate vocabulary for meetings and presentations.

lessons.title: Making Appointments
lessons.culturalNote: Scheduling business or formal meetings.

phrases.marathi: उद्या दुपारी दोन वाजता चालेल का?
phrases.transliteration: Udyaa dupaari don vaajataa chaalel ka?
phrases.english: Will tomorrow afternoon at 2 PM work?

modules.title: Culture & Celebration
modules.description: Festivals, blessings, hospitality customs, popular idioms

lessons.title: Traditional Festivals
lessons.culturalNote: Maharashtra celebrates Ganesh Utsav (Ganeshotsav) with massive grandeur. Diwali and Gudhi Padwa are also major.

lessons.title: Offering Blessings
lessons.culturalNote: Giving well-wishes or elder blessings, highly valued in Marathi homes.

phrases.english: Grow very big! (May you achieve success!)

lessons.title: Hospitality Customs
lessons.culturalNote: Maharashtrian guest etiquette: offering tea, sitting respectfully.

phrases.english: Please sit, will you have some tea?
Don't leave without eating a meal
Do come to our home for sure
Phone us once you reach home

lessons.title: Idioms & Cultural Sayings
lessons.culturalNote: Marathi is rich in 'mhani' (sayings/idioms) reflecting rustic wisdom and humor.

phrases.english: Excess of anything leads to ruin (lit: excess leads to soil/dust)
Between the devil and the deep blue sea (lit: ditch on one side, well on other)
As you sow, so shall you reap
Truth needs no proof (lit: why a mirror for a bangle on the hand?)
Forgetting the benefactor once the need is met (lit: once need ends, doctor dies)
A foolish adviser (lit: wise man sitting on a camel)
Distance lends enchantment to the view (lit: hills look beautiful from afar)

lessons.title: Congratulations
lessons.culturalNote: Celebrating achievements, promotions, or winning scores.

phrases.english: Sweeten your mouth (eat sweets to celebrate)
You did it! / You proved it!
To reach the peak of success
